title: Health & Emergencies
shortSummary: Describe symptoms, ask for help, and handle urgent situations in Hindi.
culturalNote: If you fall ill in India, pharmacies (मेडिकल स्टोर or chemist) are everywhere and pharmacists often advise on minor ailments. Many people use both modern medicine and traditional systems such as Ayurveda, and home remedies like ginger-and-tulsi tea for a cold are common. The all-India emergency number is 112, while 102 and 108 reach ambulance services in many states. When you need help, calling out 'madad kijiye' (please help) brings people quickly, as crowds tend to gather and assist. Doctors are addressed respectfully as doctor sahib or doctor-ji. It helps to learn body-part words and the phrase 'mujhe ... mein dard hai' (I have pain in my ...) so you can explain symptoms clearly even with limited Hindi.

grammar.explanation: To say a body part hurts, use mere/meri plus the body part plus mein dard hai (literally 'in my ... there is pain'). The body part takes mein (in), and the possessive agrees with it: mere sir mein, mere pet mein.

examples.native: मेरे गले में दर्द है। (mere gale mein dard hai.)
मेरे पैर में दर्द है। (mere pair mein dard hai.)
examples.en: I have pain in my leg.

grammar.commonMistake: Saying main dard hai; the pain is located in a body part with mein, not in the speaker directly.

grammar.explanation: Illnesses and sensations use mujhe (to me) plus the condition plus hai. Mujhe bukhaar hai means 'I have a fever' (literally 'to me there is fever'). The same pattern gives mujhe khaansi hai (I have a cough).
grammar.commonMistake: Using main (I) instead of mujhe (to me) for illnesses.

dialogue.setup: Emma feels unwell and speaks to a pharmacist, Dr. Mehta, at a chemist shop.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.native: नमस्ते। मुझे अच्छा नहीं लग रहा।
lines.transliteration: namaste. mujhe achha nahin lag raha.

lines.speaker: Dr. Mehta
lines.native: क्या हुआ? आपको क्या तकलीफ़ है?
lines.transliteration: kya hua? aapko kya takleef hai?
lines.english: What happened? What is troubling you?

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.native: मुझे बुखार है और सिर में दर्द है।
lines.transliteration: mujhe bukhaar hai aur sir mein dard hai.
lines.english: I have a fever and a headache.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.native: कल रात से। मुझे दवा चाहिए।
lines.transliteration: kal raat se. mujhe dawa chahiye.
lines.english: Since last night. I need medicine.

lines.speaker: Dr. Mehta
lines.native: यह दवा लीजिए, और आराम कीजिए।
lines.transliteration: yah dawa lijiye, aur aaraam kijiye.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.native: धन्यवाद। अगर ठीक न हुआ तो डॉक्टर के पास जाऊँगी।
lines.transliteration: dhanyavaad. agar theek na hua to doctor ke paas jaaungi.
lines.english: Thank you. If I don't get better, I'll go to a doctor.

quiz.question: How do you shout 'Please help!' in Hindi?
quiz.explanation: Madad means 'help' and kijiye is the polite request form.

quiz.question: Which sentence means 'I have a fever'?
quiz.explanation: Mujhe bukhaar hai is 'to me there is a fever'.

quiz.question: मेरे सिर _____ दर्द है। (I have a headache)
quiz.explanation: The body part takes mein (in) in the pain construction.

quiz.question: What does 'अस्पताल कहाँ है? (aspataal kahaan hai?)' ask?
quiz.explanation: Aspataal means 'hospital' and kahaan hai asks 'where is it'.

quiz.question: Arrange: चाहिए / मुझे / डॉक्टर (I need a doctor)
quiz.explanation: Mujhe, the noun doctor, then chahiye at the end.
